title: Products of Chern Classes of Matroid Tautological Bundles (continued)
speaker: Alex Fink
abstract:
In 2008, looking to bound the face vectors of tropical linear spaces, Speyer introduced the g-invariant of a matroid, defined in terms of exterior powers of tautological bundles on Grassmannians. He proved its coefficients nonnegative for matroids representable in characteristic zero and conjectured this in general. In a pair of recent papers, with Kris Shaw and David Speyer we reduce the question to positivity of the top coefficient, and with Andy Berget we then prove the conjecture for this coefficient.
This pair of talks will aim at presenting, with context, the central combinatorial computation of my proof with Berget: a computation by the fan displacement rule of the product of the total Chern classes of two of the tautological quotient bundles of Berget--Eur--Spink--Tseng. Strata in these intersections are governed by the external activity of a pair of matroids. This provides the relationship between the definition of the g-invariant and the external activity complex that we rely on for handling the nonrepresentable case.

proof_stages.label: Realizable degeneration recalled and combinatorial target
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the previous realizable proof, where a cap-collapsing degeneration produced a squarefree monomial ideal, and identifies the need for a purely matroidal external activity complex.

proof_stages.label: Diagonal Dilworth truncation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker constructs D(M_1,M_2) from two matroids, first by a realizable codimension-two arrangement and generic quotient, then by an independent-set polytope formula and by its independent sets and circuits.

proof_stages.label: Circuit determinant equations and initial monomials
proof_stages.summary: For every circuit of the diagonal Dilworth truncation, a block determinant built from the two realizations and the x/y variables gives the equations of the corresponding �hat Y variety, and a weight w selects one initial monomial from each such determinant.

proof_stages.label: External activity complex and generalized activity
proof_stages.summary: The external activity complex �Delta_w is defined by its w-maximal colored circuit nonfaces, and its facets are described using a generalized one/two-activity statistic from fundamental circuits.

proof_stages.label: K-polynomial via Chern-class intersections
proof_stages.summary: The computation of the K-polynomial is motivated through the geometric method and free resolutions, reducing it to products of Chern classes represented as Minkowski weights and intersected by the fan displacement rule using w.

proof_stages.label: Chern classes as nested Minkowski weights
proof_stages.summary: Using the Berget–Eur–Tseng description, a permutohedral cone contributes weight one to the relevant Chern class exactly when the successive minors in its flag are rank-one uniforms or loops, with the loop count determining the Chern degree.

proof_stages.label: Intersection graphs and the facet theorem
proof_stages.summary: Displaced Chern-class intersections are encoded by graphs whose edge slopes are fixed by w; each intersection point p gives a monomial m_p, and �Delta_w is the downward closure of all such monomials, with forests encoding bases and activity.

proof_stages.label: Valuativity and transfer to arbitrary matroids
proof_stages.summary: The finely graded K-polynomial/Hilbert series of the external activity complex is valuative in each matroid, so the realizable Cohen–Macaulay, free-resolution, and Alexander-dual consequences transfer to the nonrealizable case.

examples.name: Identity with the rank-one uniform matroid
examples.note: Taking one input to be U_{1,n} makes the diagonal Dilworth operation return the other matroid, illustrating the definition and its relation to the classical matroid Schubert case.
examples.engagement_reason: He verifies from the independent-set definition that pairing any matroid with U_{1,n} returns the original matroid, considering independent and dependent sets.

examples.name: Ordinary external activity as the U_{1,n} special case
examples.note: Setting the second matroid to U_{1,n} recovers the usual external activity convention and the Ardila–Boocher external activity complex, showing that the new activity statistic extends the classical one.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the construction to the U_{1,n} case and derives the usual external activity rule, with the minimum circuit element becoming the unique x-colored element.

examples.name: Rank-three flag with pulled-off loops
examples.note: A flag such as {1}⊂{1,…,5}⊂{1,…,10} is modified by pulling off elements like 10, 9, and 5, illustrating how loops appear in the Minkowski-weight description of lower Chern classes.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs a rank-three flag example on a line of coordinates and explicitly pulls off elements as loops to show how a lower Chern class point is formed.

examples.name: Intersection tree with a rank-two matroid having a parallel pair
examples.note: The drawn intersection graph, essentially a tree, is used to show why adding one element creates a unique cycle whose orientation yields the partition and activity data, revealing the mechanism behind the facet theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He builds and analyzes an intersection-graph/tree example involving a rank-two matroid with 2 parallel to 5, including the associated monomial and cycle/partition reasoning.
